title: Illinois fraternal benefit certificate and premarital marriage-consideration promise
source_type: calibration source excerpt derived from an Illinois appellate fraternal-benefit dispute
provenance: Curated research fixture summarizing the legally material facts from the source case used for internal Statute-of-Frauds calibration. It is not a full opinion text and should not be cited as an external legal authority without replacing it with the complete source.
calibration_role: Primary internal live-run calibration case for Frank, Karthic, Dasha perturbation behavior, and source-to-score pipeline mechanics.
summary: A fraternal benefit association member promised his fiancee before marriage that he would change his benefit certificate to name her as beneficiary. After marriage, he obtained a certificate naming her and she kept it. Later, after the marriage deteriorated, he told the association he could not produce that certificate and obtained a replacement certificate naming his children and sister. After his death, the wife and later-named beneficiaries disputed entitlement to the death benefit.
material_facts: The promise was made before marriage and conditioned on marriage.
The disputed benefit is a fraternal benefit association death benefit.
The member later obtained a certificate naming the wife as beneficiary.
The wife retained possession of the certificate naming her.
The association rules permitted a replacement certificate when the prior certificate could not be produced.
The replacement certificate named the member's children and sister.
The death-benefit dispute is between the wife and later-named beneficiaries.
expected_legal_pressure_points: Whether the premarital promise is an agreement made upon consideration of marriage.
Whether the later benefit certificate is a sufficient writing or substitute for the premarital promise.
Whether association certificate and replacement rules defeat or preserve the wife's claim.
Whether equitable theories such as constructive trust or reliance should affect the result between rival claimants.
limitations: This fixture is a compact source excerpt, not a complete reported opinion.
The fixture supports internal calibration and reproducibility, not broad Statute-of-Frauds validity by itself.
Future publication-facing runs should replace or supplement this fixture with complete, citable source materials and held-out cases.
